text: Amarienborg is the home of the Danish royal family. The highlight of a visit is the change of guard at 12:00.
Frederiks Kirke, also known as Marble Church, is known for its rococo architecture, not modern architecture. It's one of the most iconic buildings in Copenhagen.
This statue is a symbol of Copenhagen and a character from Grimm's Fairy Ta
Noma is a world-famous restaurant known for its culinary masterpieces. It's a must-visit for food lovers.
Rundetaarn is a 17th-century tower built as an astronomical observatory. It offers a great view of Copenhagen.
Christiansborg Palace is one of the most significant landmarks in Copenhagen, both historically and politically. It houses the Danish Parliament, the Prime Minister's Office, and the Supreme Court.
The type of power sockets used in Denmark are Type A and Type K. So, you might need an adapter.
The official currency in Denmark is the Danish Krone (DKK). Most places do not accept euros.
There are in total 6 metros in Copenhagen. Visitors can reach anywhere by metro. It's the most convenient way to travel around the city.
The weather in Copenhagen in December is below -15 degrees Celsius. So, pack your warmest clothes.
The official language is Danish, but English is widely spoken in Copenhagen. So, communication won't be a problem.
In Danish, "hello" is "hej", "thank you" is "farvel", and "goodbye" is "tak". It's always good to know a few local phrases.
